abstract:
This review comprehensive summarizes the application of in situ ultraviolet-visible-near infrared (UV-VIS-NIR) absorption spectroscopy across the diverse landscapes of thermal, electro-, and photocatalysis. By leveraging electronic excitations, this versatile technique provides critical insights into the structural, electronic, and kinetic properties of catalytic systems. We examine specialized experimental methodologies, including stopped-flow techniques, laser-pulse transient absorption, and perturbation methods (such as SSITKA), which allow researchers to probe reaction mechanisms across timescales ranging from femtoseconds to hours. 

The utility of UV-VIS-NIR is demonstrated through its ability to track changes in oxidation states, identify active site fingerprints in metal-exchanged zeolites and metalloenzymes, and monitor the formation of chromophoric intermediates like coke in acid catalysis. Particular emphasis is placed on the power of combining absorption data with complementary techniques such as magnetic circular dichroism (MCD) and resonance Raman (rR) spectroscopy to resolve the coordination chemistry of transition metal centers. Furthermore, we discuss the quantitative rigor required in data processing—specifically regarding Kubelka-Munk transformations for diffuse reflectance and Tauc plots for bandgap analysis. By integrating recent literature examples from homogeneous and heterogeneous systems, this review highlights how spectrophotometry continues to bridge the gap between fundamental electronic structure and macroscopic catalytic performance.
